data.title: Beyond Meat (BYND) Profit Swing To US$0.84 EPS Tests Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Beyond Meat (BYND) has rounded out FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$61.6 million and basic EPS of US$0.84, capping a twelve month stretch where total revenue was US$275.5 million and basic EPS on a trailing basis came in at US$1.15. Over recent quarters, revenue has moved from US$76.7 million in Q4 2024 to US$61.6 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS has shifted from a loss of US$0.65 in Q4 2024 to EPS of US$0.84 in Q4 2025. The story now turns to how durable these margins prove to be against softer growth expectations.

See our full analysis for Beyond Meat.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how this swing in profitability lines up with the dominant narratives around Beyond Meat, and where the numbers start to push back on those stories.

See what the community is saying about Beyond Meat

Advertisement

Profitability Turns Around While Revenue Plateaus

  • On a trailing basis, Beyond Meat moved from a loss of US$160.3 million in Q4 2024 to net income of US$178.7 million by Q4 2025, while trailing revenue was broadly flat at around US$275.5 million to US$326.5 million and is described as growing about 0.3% per year.
  • Consensus narrative points to cost cuts and portfolio changes as key levers to push toward sustained profitability, yet the modest trailing revenue movement and forecasts for slower revenue growth than the wider US market highlight that any long run earnings story needs to be built more on margins than on sales expansion.
    • Analysts expect revenue to decline by about 0.1% per year over the next three years, which sits in clear tension with bulls who lean on distribution rebuilds and new products to support revenue recovery.
    • The shift to profitability on trailing numbers aligns with the focus on efficiency, but the lack of strong revenue growth in the data keeps the long term consensus view cautious around how durable this profit swing might be.

Low 1.6x P/E Vs Food Peers

  • The trailing P/E of 1.6x sits far below the reported 20.7x average for the US Food industry and 23x for peers, even though earnings turned positive on a trailing basis and EPS over the last twelve months is US$1.15.
  • Bulls argue that operational improvements and a pivot toward cleaner, higher protein products could justify a stronger earnings profile, but the current valuation gap is paired with limited top line growth and high non cash earnings.
    • Trailing revenue growth running at about 0.3% per year contrasts with bullish scenarios that rely on stronger retail velocity and expanded shelf space to support higher sales.
    • The presence of high non cash earnings and negative equity in the risk summary means the low P/E may reflect quality concerns about the earnings base rather than a simple mispricing that bullish investors can rely on.
Bulls who see the 1.6x P/E as an opening should weigh it against the quality of earnings and balance sheet signals set out in the full bull case 🐂 Beyond Meat Bull Case.

Balance Sheet Strain And Dilution Risks

  • Despite the move to trailing profitability, the company is flagged as having negative shareholders' equity, weak debt coverage by operating cash flow, and substantial share dilution over the past year, while the share price around US$0.62 sits far below the consensus analyst target of US$1.61.
  • Bears highlight that persistent cash burn and leverage could pressure equity holders, and the risk summary around dilution and volatility shows how financial structure issues can matter as much as the income statement.
    • The combination of negative equity and debt not well covered by operating cash flow is consistent with the bearish concern that ongoing losses at the cash level keep refinancing and dilution very much on the table.
    • Share price volatility over the past three months, plus the need for analysts to assume share count growth of 7% per year in their models, supports the cautious view that existing holders carry meaningful dilution and pricing risk even after the recent profitability shift.
Skeptics focused on cash flow, equity erosion, and share issuance can see how those themes fit into the more cautious case around the stock in the bear thesis 🐻 Beyond Meat Bear Case

    • Over the same trailing period, net income swung from US$1.5 million in Q3 2024 to a loss of US$3.1 million by Q3 2025, so the softer top line is sitting alongside an absence of net profit.

  • Across the trailing 12 months to Q3 2025, net income moved from a profit of US$1.5 million in Q3 2024 to a loss of US$3.1 million, while basic EPS shifted from US$0.10 to a loss of US$0.17 per share over the same window.
  • Critics highlight this shift in profitability as a core bearish argument, and the quarterly figures give clear support for that concern:
    • In the last five reported quarters, JFB has only shown small quarterly profits of about US$0.03 million to US$0.35 million in Q2 and Q4 2024 and Q1 2025, compared with losses of US$0.37 million, US$2.4 million and US$1.1 million in the other periods.
    • The latest Q2 and Q3 2025 losses, at US$2.4 million and US$1.1 million respectively, sit against lower revenue quarters of US$3.7 million and US$5.0 million, so bears see a business that has not yet matched its cost base to its current revenue level.
Skeptical investors often ask whether this earnings profile is the start of a longer loss making stretch or a temporary stumble, and the detailed bear case breaks down those concerns in more depth in the 🐻 JFB Construction Holdings Bear Case.

  • JFB trades on a P/S of 6.9x, which the analysis flags as lower than a 19x peer group average but higher than the wider US Construction industry at 1.4x, so the shares sit between direct peers and the broader sector on this single valuation metric.
  • What stands out for bearish investors is how this valuation sits against the current fundamentals, given the company is unprofitable and has seen shareholder dilution:
    • The trailing 12 month numbers show no net profit and a US$3.1 million net loss, so any P/S comparison is being made on revenue alone rather than earnings support.
    • Shareholders also experienced dilution over the past year and the share price has been described as highly volatile over the last three months, which bears see as extra risk layered on top of the mixed valuation signal.

data.description:
Crypto and blockchain stocks sit at the intersection of technology, finance, and changing market plumbing, which can be appealing when geopolitical tensions, energy prices, and bond yields keep shifting. With manufacturing data mixed across regions and input costs under pressure, many investors are looking beyond traditional sectors to themes tied to digital infrastructure and alternative transaction systems. Cipher Digital (CIFR)

Overview: Cipher Digital develops and runs large scale data centers in the United States that are used primarily for Bitcoin mining today and are being adapted for high performance computing hosting, serving hyperscale tenants that need significant power and cooling capacity for AI and other compute intensive workloads.

Operations: Cipher Digital currently generates all of its US$223.9 million in revenue from Bitcoin mining operations in the United States.

Market Cap: US$5.21b

Cipher Digital is shifting from being a pure Bitcoin miner to an owner of long term AI and high performance computing infrastructure, with 15 year campus leases, a 600 MW pipeline for hyperscale tenants such as Google and AWS, and low cost power contracts that support margin potential. Analysts have highlighted the company’s revenue and earnings outlook, yet it remains unprofitable, trades at a high P/S multiple, and relies on external funding while spending heavily on new hardware and sites. Combined with share price volatility, insider selling and uncertainty around how quickly AI leases are taken up, this results in a higher risk stock that may appeal to investors who understand both the potential benefits and these pressure points.

Cipher Digital’s pivot from pure Bitcoin mining to long term AI and high performance computing hosting could be masking a much bigger story about risk, funding and contract quality. Start with the 1 key reward and 3 important warning signs (1 is major!) and see what might be hiding in plain sight.

Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR)

Overview: Bitmine Immersion Technologies runs a blockchain focused business that centers on Ethereum and Bitcoin, providing consulting and advisory services, equipment leasing, hosting and power optimization, and digital asset treasury management while gradually stepping back from running its own mining sites. The company also sells mining equipment and increasingly positions itself around institutional grade Ethereum staking and broader digital asset ecosystem services.

Operations: Bitmine Immersion Technologies generates US$7.19 million in revenue from the cryptocurrency mining industry, all from the United States.

Market Cap: US$9.0b

Bitmine Immersion Technologies may appeal to investors who want direct exposure to Ethereum infrastructure, rather than just token price moves, through a business that is building out staking services and ecosystem support around a very large ETH treasury. Bitmine Immersion Technologies is trying to turn a complex Ethereum focused balance sheet into an opportunity, but the real story sits in the funding mix, treasury risk and US$9.0b valuation relative to its US$7.19 million in revenue. Get the full picture in the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs (1 is major!)

Galaxy Digital (GLXY)

Overview: Galaxy Digital is a diversified digital asset group that runs trading, lending, asset management, advisory and blockchain infrastructure services, alongside data centers, bitcoin mining, and a GalaxyOne platform that connects individual investors to both traditional and crypto markets.

Operations: Galaxy Digital generates about US$61.2 million in revenue primarily from its Digital Assets segment, with smaller contributions from Treasury and Corporate at roughly US$99.2 million and Data Centers at about US$7.2 million. The company reports all revenue out of Canada.

Galaxy Digital provides access to institutional-grade crypto trading, asset management and staking services, and also ties in large-scale Helios data center capacity that links digital assets with AI and high performance computing demand. For you as a shareholder or prospective investor, this is less about the eventual size of the allocation and more about what it signals. After a year where SoFi has faced a Muddy Waters short report, a sharp year to date share price decline, and questions around its accounting, confirmation from a high profile issuer can help counter the idea that institutional partners are stepping back from the platform. It also sits alongside SoFi’s FTSE All World Index inclusion and the US$3.6b expansion of its Loan Platform Business, reinforcing the picture of a company that is still plugged into major capital-market flows even while sentiment around the stock has been volatile.

How This Fits Into The SoFi Technologies Narrative

  • At the same time, the focus on IPO access could distract from concerns already raised in the narrative about competition, regulation, and how much optimism is embedded in expectations for user and revenue growth.
  • The narrative discusses capital raises, fee based growth, and macro risks, but it does not fully factor in how recurring access to marquee IPOs might influence member engagement or SoFi’s appeal relative to other fintechs.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for SoFi Technologies to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Access to a SpaceX IPO could raise expectations that SoFi will regularly secure similar deals, so any smaller than hoped allocation or future exclusion may disappoint active users.
  • ⚠️ The ongoing Muddy Waters allegations about accounting and non cash earnings still sit in the background, so sentiment around governance and financial reporting can remain fragile even if IPO access improves brand perception.
  • 🎁 Continued inclusion in high profile deals supports the idea that SoFi is a relevant channel for issuers and institutions, which may help retention and cross sell across its broader banking and investing products.
  • 🎁 Combined with index inclusion and growth in the capital light Loan Platform Business, this update adds to the case that SoFi is building multiple touchpoints with both retail investors and large financial partners, not relying on a single product line.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching whether SoFi provides any concrete details on SpaceX IPO access, such as member eligibility criteria or allocation mechanics, and how that compares with platforms like Robinhood or E*TRADE. Pay attention to any impact on new account openings and engagement metrics around major offerings, especially against the backdrop of short seller scrutiny and upcoming quarterly results. The bigger picture question is whether SoFi can keep turning headline access and new partnerships into durable, fee based activity rather than one off trading spikes.

data.title: Is MakeMyTrip (NasdaqGS:MMYT) Pricing Fair After Recent Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether MakeMyTrip at around US$37.88 is a bargain or just looks cheap, the key question is how its current price compares with its underlying worth.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with a 3.1% decline over the last 7 days, a 32.3% decline over 30 days, and year to date returns of a 53.7% decline, although the 3 year and 5 year returns sit at 61.7% and 26.6% respectively.
  • This price action comes as MakeMyTrip continues to sit in focus as a Nasdaq listed online travel player. Investors are weighing up long term demand for digital travel bookings against changing risk appetite in growth oriented names. Broader sentiment around US listed consumer services companies has also shaped how quickly investors are willing to reprice expectations.
  • Right now, MakeMyTrip holds a value score of 1 out of 6. This raises useful questions about how different valuation checks, from cash flow models to multiples, compare with each other and how a more complete way of thinking about valuation comes together at the end of this article.

MakeMyTrip scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: MakeMyTrip Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return.

For MakeMyTrip, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$208 million. Analyst inputs and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St feed into ten year projections, with free cash flow for 2035 estimated at around US$492 million.

Rolling all of these projected cash flows together, the DCF model arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$40.75 per share. Compared with the recent share price of roughly US$37.88, this implies the stock is trading at around a 7.1% discount to this estimate, which sits in the “close but slightly cheap” zone rather than a clear-cut bargain.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

MakeMyTrip is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MakeMyTrip.

Approach 2: MakeMyTrip Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It links directly to the bottom line, which is usually where equity investors focus first.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk often calls for a lower one.

MakeMyTrip currently trades on a P/E of 63.32x. That sits above the Hospitality industry average of 20.62x and also above the broader peer average of 24.87x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for MakeMyTrip is 52.19x, which reflects a preferred P/E that factors in elements such as earnings growth, profit margin, market cap, industry and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own profile rather than assuming that all Hospitality names deserve the same multiple. With the current P/E at 63.32x versus a Fair Ratio of 52.19x, the shares screen as OVERVALUED on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Park Street (CPSE:PARKST A) EPS Surge To DKK0.41 Tests Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Park Street (CPSE:PARKST A) has reported FY 2025 first half revenue of DKK73.7m and basic EPS of DKK0.41, with the trailing twelve months showing revenue of DKK142.5m and EPS of DKK0.50 as the company trades around DKK15.70 per share. Over recent periods, revenue has moved from DKK82.3m in the first half of FY 2024 to DKK68.9m in the second half and DKK73.7m in the first half of FY 2025, while basic EPS has shifted from DKK0.10 to DKK0.07 and then to DKK0.41. This sets the scene for a closer look at how margins are shaping the story around profitability quality.

See our full analysis for Park Street.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around Park Street's growth, risks, and earnings quality, and where those stories might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

DKK21.7m profit heavily helped by DKK29.3m one off

  • Over the last 12 months Park Street recorded DKK21.7m in net income excluding extra items, while the trailing result also included a DKK29.3m one off gain that had a big impact on reported profit.
  • What stands out for a more cautious, bearish view is that, even with DKK21.7m of underlying profit in the trailing 12 months, reported earnings have declined by 63.8% per year over five years. This raises questions about how much of the recent profitability is driven by items like the DKK29.3m gain rather than a broad improvement in the core business.
    • That five year earnings decline sits uncomfortably next to the recent return to profit, so bears can point to a long stretch of weaker performance versus a single profitable year that is helped by non recurring income.
    • For you as an investor, this combination makes it important to separate the DKK21.7m of trailing net income from the DKK29.3m one off when thinking about how repeatable these results might be.

EPS swing from DKK0.07 to DKK0.41 in six months

  • Basic EPS moved from DKK0.07 in the second half of FY 2024 to DKK0.41 in the first half of FY 2025, and on a trailing 12 month basis sits at DKK0.50, even though five year reported earnings have fallen by 63.8% per year.
  • Supporters of a more bullish angle can point out that Park Street has at least moved into positive EPS territory over the last year. However, this is tempered by the fact that the EPS level relied on the same period that contained the DKK29.3m one off gain and comes after several years of shrinking reported earnings.
    • This mix of a sharp EPS jump in the most recent half and a long history of declining reported earnings means short term numbers look strong on the surface but do not fully counter the longer term trend.
    • If you are thinking about longer holding periods, the contrast between DKK0.50 trailing EPS and the multi year decline highlights why it helps to track more than a single period headline.

To see how other investors are interpreting this swing in profitability and what they think could come next, it is worth checking out the broader discussion around Park Street in the community narratives Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

P/E of 31.4x sits between peers and wider sector

  • At around DKK15.70 per share, the stock trades on a trailing P/E of 31.4x, which is below the peer average of 49.3x but above the wider European Real Estate industry average of 13.2x.
  • Critics highlight that this valuation multiple is being asked of a company whose reported earnings have fallen by 63.8% per year over five years and where interest payments are not well covered by earnings. As a result, the current P/E of 31.4x is being compared against both weaker multi year profit trends and ongoing pressure on interest coverage.
    • The weak coverage of interest expense means a meaningful portion of operating profit is being absorbed by financing costs, which cautious investors often factor in when deciding whether a premium to the industry average P/E is comfortable.
    • At the same time, the move to profitability over the last year is the main identified reward in the trailing data, so some investors may see the 31.4x P/E as reflecting that improvement even as they weigh it against the longer running decline in reported earnings.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on Park Street's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

data.title: IAC’s Larger MGM Stake Puts Governance And Capital Allocation In Focus
data.description:
• IAC/InterActiveCorp, led by Barry Diller, has increased its stake in MGM Resorts International to more than 23%.
  • This move makes IAC the largest shareholder of MGM Resorts International, reinforcing its role in the company’s ownership structure.
  • The development highlights fresh attention on NYSE:MGM and how large shareholders may influence future decisions.

MGM Resorts International, trading under NYSE:MGM, last closed at $36.78. The stock shows a 19.6% return over the past year, while longer multiyear returns such as the past 3 and 5 years have been negative. That mix of outcomes gives investors a range of data points to weigh alongside this latest ownership shift.

IAC’s larger position is likely to keep investor focus on how major shareholders engage with MGM’s governance and long term plans. Readers may want to watch for any changes in board dynamics, capital allocation choices, or messaging from both companies as this relationship evolves.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for MGM Resorts International by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on MGM Resorts International.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling MGM Resorts International following this latest news.

IAC raising its stake past 23% signals that a long term oriented investor is comfortable tying more capital to MGM at current levels. For you, that sort of conviction from a large, influential shareholder can matter because IAC is not just a passive holder, it has a track record of engaging with portfolio companies on digital strategy and capital allocation. With MGM already active on share repurchases and product experiments such as the all inclusive bundle on the south Strip, a larger IAC presence may keep attention on how management prioritises buybacks, new projects and balance sheet risk. It also concentrates ownership further, which can amplify IAC’s voice relative to smaller holders when topics like board composition or potential partnerships come up. On the flip side, concentrated ownership means the investor base leans more heavily on the outlook and preferences of one group, which may not always align with every minority shareholder. Overall, this development gives you another reference point when weighing governance quality, decision making and how outside capital providers view MGM at a share price around US$36.78.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The MGM Resorts International Narrative

  • The larger IAC position lines up with the existing focus on capital allocation flexibility. A committed anchor shareholder can support management when weighing buybacks, international projects and digital growth against leverage and cash flow.
  • Greater influence from a single investor could challenge the balance between pursuing premium focused growth initiatives and keeping risk in check, especially with sizable commitments to projects like Osaka or Dubai already highlighted in the narrative.
  • The narrative emphasises operating strategy and long dated projects more than ownership structure. This means the implications of one shareholder controlling more than 23% of MGM may not yet be fully reflected in how governance and decision making are framed.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for MGM Resorts International to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that MGM carries a high level of debt, so if IAC encourages more aggressive capital returns or large projects without offsetting cash flow, financial flexibility could be pressured.
  • ⚠️ Profit margins of 1.2% are lower than last year at 4.3%, which means any push for faster change in strategy from a major shareholder happens from a relatively thin profitability base.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow 15.23% per year, and a supportive long term shareholder can provide stability as management works through that earnings plan alongside investments in resorts and digital betting.
  • 🎁 IAC’s larger stake may signal confidence in MGM’s broader hospitality and gaming portfolio versus peers such as Caesars Entertainment, Wynn Resorts or Las Vegas Sands, which some investors may see as a positive read on the company’s positioning.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on any filings that show further IAC buying or changes in its intentions, as well as whether IAC gains additional board representation. Listen for references to this shareholder in MGM’s commentary on capital allocation, especially around buybacks and large projects. It is also worth tracking how other big holders respond, for example whether ownership becomes more concentrated or more dispersed over time, and how that feeds into sentiment relative to peers like Caesars and Wynn. Any shift in governance structure, capital return policies or public messaging that is linked to IAC’s involvement will help you judge how this larger stake is shaping the MGM story.
